intro:
Antiochus IV Epiphanes (c. 215-164 BC), eighth king of the Seleucid dynasty, is the most prominent Gentile villain of the intertestamental period. His self-given epithet Epiphanes ('God manifest') expressed the Hellenistic ruler cult that shaped his policies; his Jewish opponents called him Epimanes ('the mad one'). His aggressive Hellenization program for Judea saw the Jerusalem high priesthood sold to the highest bidder: Jason and then Menelaus purchased the office through bribery. In 167 BC Antiochus plundered the temple treasury, erected an altar to Zeus Olympios in the sanctuary, sacrificed pigs on the altar, and forbade circumcision, Sabbath observance, and possession of the Torah under penalty of death — atrocities collectively termed 'the abomination of desolation' in 1 Maccabees 1:54 and Daniel 11:31.

These persecutions triggered the Maccabean revolt under Mattathias and his sons, culminating in the rededication of the temple in 164 BC — the event commemorated in Hanukkah. The Book of Daniel's visionary passages are widely understood as addressing Antiochus's persecution: the 'little horn' of Daniel 8:9-14 whose desecration lasts 2,300 evenings and mornings is interpreted by most scholars as referring primarily to Antiochus, even as Jesus applies the 'abomination of desolation' language from Daniel to a future fulfillment (Matt. 24:15; Mark 13:14). Antiochus died during a campaign in Persia in 164 BC. His career established the apocalyptic template of a supreme anti-God tyrant whose assault on the covenant community precedes divine deliverance.
